critique.summary: The article describes 'Get Shit Done' (GSD), a meta-prompting, context engineering, and spec-driven development system designed to improve the reliability and quality of AI-generated code using Claude Code and other AI runtimes. It emphasizes a streamlined workflow that avoids enterprise complexity and focuses on clear project phases, automated verification, and atomic commits.
mainPoints: GSD is a lightweight system that enhances AI coding with context engineering to prevent quality degradation as AI fills its context window.
It supports multiple AI runtimes including Claude Code, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Codex, Copilot, and Antigravity.
The system uses a structured workflow with phases: project initialization, discussion, planning, execution, verification, and milestone completion.
GSD employs multi-agent orchestration to parallelize tasks and maintain fresh context windows for consistent output quality.
Each task results in atomic git commits for traceability and easy debugging.
The system is designed for solo developers or small teams who want to avoid complex enterprise processes.
It includes features like XML prompt formatting, automated verification, and flexible configuration for different project needs.
GSD is open source, MIT licensed, and has a large community with thousands of stars and forks on GitHub.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about GSD's features and workflow are consistent with the publicly available information on its official GitHub repository. The project is real, actively maintained, and widely used as indicated by its GitHub statistics and community engagement. The description of its functionality aligns with typical practices in AI-assisted coding tools and spec-driven development. There are no apparent false or misleading statements in the article.
considerations: The article does not provide independent third-party reviews or comparisons with similar tools, which would help assess its effectiveness objectively.
It assumes familiarity with AI coding assistants and may not address limitations or challenges users might face.
Security considerations mention protecting sensitive files but do not elaborate on potential risks of automated code generation.
The scalability and performance of GSD in large or complex projects are not discussed in detail.
User testimonials are quoted without attribution, so their authenticity cannot be independently verified.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the effectiveness and efficiency of the GSD framework compared to alternatives like Superpowers and PAUL. Users share mixed experiences regarding token usage, output quality, and practical utility, with some praising GSD's productivity boost and others criticizing it as overengineered or token-heavy. Concerns about code review, security, and real-world applicability are also raised.

comments.author: yoaviram
comments.text:
I've been using GSD extensively over the past 3 months. I previously used speckit, which I found lacking. GSD consistently gets me 95% of the way there on complex tasks. That's amazing. The last 5% is mostly "manual" testing. We've used GSD to build and launch a SaaS product including an agent-first CMS (whiteboar.it).

It's hard to say why GSD worked so much better for us than other similar frameworks, because the underlying models also improved considerably during the same period. What is clear is that it's a huge productivity boost over vanilla Claude Code.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed personal experience with GSD, highlighting its strengths and acknowledging limitations, making it a credible and balanced perspective.

comments.author: Andrei_dev
comments.text:
250K lines in a month — okay, but what does review actually look like at that volume?

I've been poking at security issues in AI-generated repos and it's the same thing: more generation means less review. Not just logic — checking what's in your .env, whether API routes have auth middleware, whether debug endpoints made it to prod.

You can move that fast. But "review" means something different now. Humans make human mistakes. AI writes clean-looking code that ships with hardcoded credentials because some template had them and nobody caught it.

All these frameworks are racing to generate faster. Nobody's solving the verification side at that speed.
comments.analysis: Raises important concerns about the risks of rapid AI-generated code production, emphasizing the need for thorough review and security checks, which is a valid and often overlooked issue.

comments.author: ibrahim_h
comments.text:
The README recommends --dangerously-skip-permissions as the intended workflow. Looking at gsd-executor.md you can see why — subagents run node gsd-tools.cjs, git checkout -b, eslint, test runners, all generated dynamically by the planner. Approving each one kills autonomous mode.

There is a gsd-plan-checker that runs before execution, but it only verifies logical completeness — requirement coverage, dependency graphs, context budget. It never looks at what commands will actually run. So if the planner generates something destructive, the plan-checker won't catch it because that's not what it checks for. The gsd-verifier runs after execution, checking whether the goal was achieved, not whether anything bad happened along the way. In /gsd:autonomous this chains across all remaining phases unattended.

The granular permissions fallback in the README only covers safe reads and git ops — but the executor needs way more than that to actually function. Feels like there should be a permission profile scoped to what GSD actually needs without going full skip.
comments.analysis: Provides a technical critique of GSD's permission and safety mechanisms, highlighting potential security risks and gaps in the current implementation.

comments.author: gtirloni
comments.text: I was using this and superpowers but eventually, Plan mode became enough and I prefer to steer Claude Code myself. These frameworks are great for fire-and-forget tasks, especially when there is some research involved but they burn 10x more tokens, in my experience. I was always hitting the Max plan limits for no discernable benefit in the outcomes I was getting. But this will vary a lot depending on how people prefer to work.
comments.analysis: Shares a practical comparison based on personal usage, noting token inefficiency and preferring manual control, which aligns with other comments about token consumption.

comments.author: MeetingsBrowser
comments.text:
I've tried it, and I'm not convinced I got measurably better results than just prompting claude code directly.

It absolutely tore through tokens though. I don't normally hit my session limits, but hit the 5-hour limits in ~30 minutes and my weekly limits by Tuesday with GSD.
comments.analysis: Echoes concerns about token usage and questions the value added by GSD compared to direct prompting, providing a skeptical but reasonable viewpoint.

comments.author: jankhg
comments.text:
Apart from GSD and superpowers, there's another system, called PAUL [1]. It apparently requires fewer tokens compared to GSD, as it does not use subagents, but keeps all in one session. A detailed comparison with GSD is part of the repo [2].

[1] https://github.com/ChristopherKahler/paul

[2] https://github.com/ChristopherKahler/paul/blob/main/PAUL-VS-...
comments.analysis: Introduces a relevant alternative with a focus on token efficiency and provides resources for further comparison, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: gbrindisi
comments.text:
I like openspec, it lets you tune the workflow to your liking and doesn’t get in the way.

I started with all the standard spec flow and as I got more confident and opinionated I simplified it to my liking.

I think the point of any spec driven framework is that you want to eventually own the workflow yourself, so that you can constraint code generation on your own terms.
comments.analysis: Offers a positive perspective on spec-driven frameworks emphasizing customization and user control, which is a valuable insight for users considering these tools.

comments.author: jatora
comments.text: Another heavily overengineered AND underengineered abomination. I'm convinced anyone who advocates for these types of tools would find just as much success just prompting claude code normally and taking a little bit to plan first. Such a waste of time to bother with these tools that solve a problem that never existed in the first place.
comments.analysis: Expresses strong skepticism and frustration, dismissing the utility of these frameworks; while emotional, the critique aligns with some users' experiences about complexity and token cost.

comments.author: dhorthy
comments.text:
it is very hard for me to take seriously any system that is not proven for shipping production code in complex codebases that have been around for a while.

I've been down the "don't read the code" path and I can say it leads nowhere good.

I am perhaps talking my own book here, but I'd like to see more tools that brag about "shipped N real features to production" or "solved Y problem in large-10-year-old-codebase"

I'm not saying that coding agents can't do these things and such tools don't exist, I'm just afraid that counting 100k+ LOC that the author didn't read kind of fuels the "this is all hype-slop" argument rather than helping people discover the ways that coding agents can solve real and valuable problems.
comments.analysis: Calls for more evidence of real-world production use and cautions against hype, a reasonable demand for maturity and credibility in AI coding tools.

comments.author: arjie
comments.text: I could not produce useful output from this. It was useful as a rubber duck because it asks good motivating questions during the plan phase, but the actual implementation was lacklustre and not worth the effort. In the end, I just have Claude Opus create plans, and then I have it write them to memory and update it as it goes along and the output is better.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced review highlighting the planning utility but criticizing the implementation quality, offering a balanced user experience.

comments.author: obsidianbases1
comments.text:
> If you know clearly what you want

This is the real challenge. The people I know that jump around to new tools have a tough time explaining what they want, and thus how new tool is better than last tool.
comments.analysis: Insightfully points out that tool effectiveness depends on user clarity of goals, a valid observation about user behavior impacting tool adoption.

comments.author: tkiolp4
comments.text: The whole gsd/agents folder is hilarious. Like a bunch of MD that never breaks. How do you is it minimally correct? Subjective prose. Sad to see this on the frontpage
comments.analysis: Critiques the documentation or code quality with sarcasm and frustration; while emotional, it raises concerns about maintainability and clarity.

comments.author: maccam912
comments.text: I've had a good experience with https://github.com/obra/superpowers. At first glance this looks similar. Has anyone tried both who can offer a comparison?
comments.analysis: Seeks comparative insights, contributing to the discussion by encouraging knowledge sharing but does not provide substantive analysis itself.

comments.author: thr0waway001
comments.text: At the risk of sounding stupid what does the author mean by: “I’m not a 50-person software company. I don’t want to play enterprise theatre.” ?
comments.analysis: Asks for clarification on a quoted phrase, showing engagement but adding little analytical value.

comments.author: prakashrj
comments.text: With GSD, I was able to write 250K lines of code in less than a month, without prior knowledge of claude.
comments.analysis: States a quantitative achievement but lacks context or detail about quality or review, limiting its usefulness.

comments.author: dfltr
comments.text: GSD has a reputation for being a token burner compared to something like Superpowers. Has that changed lately? Always open to revisiting things as they improve.
comments.analysis: Poses a question about recent improvements, showing interest in updates but not providing analysis.

comments.author: greenchair
comments.analysis: Dismisses the project based solely on its name without substantive reasoning, contributing little to the discussion.
